text: Does the coupled Burgers-swept-KdV system, evolved from u(x,0)=0, v(x,0)=4*exp(-(x+5)^2/2.25) on x in [-15,15] with periodic BC up to T=6.0, decompose the Gaussian wave packet in v into a train of >=2 well-separated solitons (each amp >=0.8) with mass(v) drift <8%?

params.rhs: u_t=-3u u_x - d/dx(3v^2+v_xx); v_t=-6v v_x - v_xxx - d/dx(uv)

bank_use_rationale: no knowledge bank (NoKB). E1 is the simplest meaningful baseline under progressive-complexity discipline: spectral spatial derivatives are required (central FD cannot resolve v_xxx at Nx=256 over a 30-unit domain), paired with explicit RK4 (no IMEX, no splitting) and no dealiasing filter. dt=5e-5 < CFL bound 2.828/|i*k_max|^3 ~ 1.5e-4 with ample margin.

diagnostics.interpretation: high-frequency aliasing cascade: 125 spurious peaks scattered across the whole domain at t=0.28 with amplitudes monotonically increasing toward the largest spectral grid cell. RK4 stability for v_xxx alone is fine; the runaway comes from aliasing in v^2 and (uv) nonlinear products feeding the v_xxx-amplified high-k modes.

rationale: Failure mode is exactly the textbook one for spectral KdV-type without dealiasing. Baseline confirms that the simplest method does NOT survive past t~0.28, so we must upgrade.
Single-component upgrade: add 2/3-rule dealiasing to the spectral RHS. Keep RK4, dt=5e-5, Nx=256 identical. This isolates whether aliasing alone is responsible for the blow-up.

method: Fourier pseudospectral + 2/3-rule dealiasing on nonlinear products + explicit RK4

params.rhs: u_t=-3*ded(u,u_x) - d/dx(3*ded(v,v) + v_xx); v_t=-6*ded(v,v_x) - v_xxx - d/dx(ded(u,v))

bank_use_rationale: no bank. Single-component upgrade over E1: only change is adding 2/3-rule dealiasing on every quadratic product (u u_x, v v_x, v^2, u v). RK4 time integrator, dt, Nx, IC all unchanged so any blow-up vs success directly attributes to aliasing as the broken component.

diagnostics.interpretation: Dealiasing eliminated the t~0.28 spectral cascade; mass(v) perfectly conserved. But between t=3.0 and t=3.5 the u-channel runs away from ~92 to ~1170. Cause: u(x,0)=0 leaves the system OFF the Gardner reduction manifold (m = u - v^2/2 = -v^2/2 ne 0); the inviscid Burgers term 3 u u_x in u-equation forms a shock with no dissipation to absorb it. v gets driven by -d/dx(u v) which then amplifies as |u| grows.

rationale: Eliminated one failure mode (aliasing) but exposed another (Burgers-shock formation in the u channel of the off-manifold dynamics). Late-time v has 62 peaks of huge amplitude rather than a clean train of ~2-3 well-separated solitons.

Single-component upgrade on top of E2: add small high-order spectral hyperviscosity (-eps k^8) to absorb the Burgers shock in u and keep gradients finite. Choose eps so that the dissipative timescale at the dealiasing cutoff k_c=(2/3)k_max is ~0.1 s; the soliton scale (sigma~1.5, hence k~4) is in the inertial range and unaffected.

method: Fourier pseudospectral + 2/3-rule dealiasing + spectral k^8 hyperviscosity + explicit RK4
bank_use_rationale: no bank. Single-component upgrade over E2: add k^8 hyperviscosity. All other components (dealiasing, RK4, dt, IC, Nx) unchanged so any change in failure mode directly attributes to the new dissipation.

rationale: Final v shows 6 well-separated peak clusters (gap > 1.5 spatial units, i.e. > one soliton width), each with cluster-peak amplitude in [2.4, 4.4] — comfortably above the 0.8 threshold. Mass(v) drift is 0.000% (well below 8%). Amplitude history is bounded between 2.1 and 4.4 throughout the run, no blow-up. Satisfies the phenomenon target with margin: >= 2 well-separated peaks each amp >= 0.8 with mass drift < 8%.

E3 produces a bounded, mass-conserving solution whose final v field exhibits multiple well-separated peaks all exceeding the 0.8 amplitude threshold, satisfying the soliton-train phenomenon target. No further iterations needed.
